title: Backup restore test evidence — RPO/RTO SLA compliance per tier
exclusions: Does NOT evaluate data-integrity of restored content; pairs with UC-22.45.2.
evidence: Weekly 'backup_restore_sla' writes RPO/RTO test evidence per tier to compliance_summary.

compliance.assurance_rationale: CP-9 system backup — the UC evidences successful test restoration.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIST 800-53 CP-9 (System backup) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.45.1: Backup restore test evidence — RPO/RTO SLA compliance per tier.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.45.1' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Art.12 backup policies and recovery methods — mandates tested RPO/RTO.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that DORA Art.12 (Backup policies and recovery methods) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.45.1: Backup restore test evidence — RPO/RTO SLA compliance per tier.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.45.1' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

compliance.assurance_rationale: A1.2 availability — backup testing is standard SOC 2 evidence.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that SOC 2 A1.2 (Availability commitments) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.45.1: Backup restore test evidence — RPO/RTO SLA compliance per tier.

controlTest.positiveScenario: When a tier-1 system misses either its RPO or RTO during the weekly test, the UC fires with system, tier, measured_rpo, measured_rto.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Tier-1 systems meeting both RPO and RTO do NOT fire.

dataSources: See subcategory preamble. — supplemented by authentication logs, system audit trails, and configuration management database (CMDB) asset inventory exports
spl:
index=backup sourcetype=restore:test earliest=-7d
| stats max(rpo_seconds) as rpo_s, max(rto_seconds) as rto_s by system tier test_id
| lookup dr_tiers.csv tier OUTPUT rpo_slo rto_slo
| where rpo_s>rpo_slo OR rto_s>rto_slo
| table system tier rpo_s rpo_slo rto_s rto_slo
description: Weekly restore-test evidence with per-tier SLOs. Any failing test becomes an audit-visible finding instead of a silently-missed DR exercise.
value: Makes backup testing a continuous control rather than an annual artefact.
implementation: (1) Configure restore test jobs; (2) ingest durations and results; (3) schedule weekly; (4) on miss, escalate to continuity lead. Indexes required: `index=backup`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=restore:test`; Lookups: `dr_tiers`, `dr_tiers.csv`.
visualization: Table of tests, line chart of RPO/RTO trend, single-value '% of tier-1 systems within SLO'.
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Authorised security assessment activities** — Planned security control assessments, continuous monitoring scans, and Plan of Action and Milestones (POA&M) validation tests generate expected control deviation signals that are part of the Assessment & Authorisation process.

2. **System authorisation boundary changes** — During ATO re-authorisation, system boundary expansions, or inheritance model updates, temporary control gaps may appear in the monitoring baseline until the updated security plan is approved by the Authorising Official.

3. **Interconnection Security Agreement (ISA) activity** — Data exchanges with authorised external systems operating under signed ISAs produce cross-boundary traffic that triggers network monitoring alerts but is covered by the formal interconnection agreement.

4. **FIPS 199 impact-level re-categorisation** — When system categorisation changes (e.g., Moderate to High), the transition period produces control baseline mismatches until the enhanced controls are fully implemented per the approved implementation schedule.

**Suppression mechanism:** Maintain a `nist_800_53_exception_filter` KV Store lookup containing approved assessment schedule entries, POA&M IDs, ISA identifiers, and system re-categorisation project IDs. The correlation search filters against this lookup and suppresses matches with valid exception entries.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Indexes**: `backup` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Lookups**: `dr_tiers.csv` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('dr_tiers.csv')`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `CP-9`. The control objective is: "Evidence that NIST 800-53 CP-9 (System backup) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.45.1: Backup restore test evidence — RPO/RTO SLA compliance per tier." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into `backup` under `restore:test`, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


Four distinct operational considerations shape this detection's rollout:


• **Hint 1:** Configure restore test jobs. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 2:** ingest durations and results. Ingestion SLA drift is usually invisible until it breaks something — monitor the feed's freshness with a companion search that alerts when the newest event is older than the expected cadence.

• **Hint 3:** schedule weekly. Schedule cadence is a tradeoff between regulatory latency and search-head load; document the chosen cadence and the reasoning in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** on miss, escalate to continuity lead. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "Backup restore test evidence — RPO/RTO SLA compliance per tier," the aggregation is the evidence artifact itself — auditors will read these rows as the official record of NIST 800-53 control activity.

This search enriches raw events with context from the lookup `dr_tiers.csv` — the enrichment table that adds compliance-relevant business context to raw events. The raw event stream alone cannot answer the compliance question because it does not carry the authoritative business attributes (`rpo_slo rto_slo`) — these live in a register maintained outside the monitored system. The `| lookup` joins at search time on `tier`, making the search results only as accurate as the lookup's freshness.

Lookup ownership and freshness: This is the single most common failure mode for lookup-based compliance searches. The `dr_tiers.csv` table must be owned by a named team with a documented refresh cadence and staleness alerting. A minimum operational SLA is: (1) lookup refreshed at a cadence matching the risk domain (daily for access control, weekly for inventory, quarterly for vendor registers), (2) automated alert when the lookup has not been rewritten in 2x the refresh interval, (3) a row-count sanity check (alert if row count changes by more than 20% between refreshes — catches truncation bugs in upstream exports).

Case sensitivity: Splunk's lookup matching is case-sensitive by default. If the raw event field and the lookup key differ in case (e.g. `Account_Name=JSMITH` vs lookup `samaccountname=jsmith`), the join silently returns null. Normalize both sides with `| eval tier = lower(tier)` before the lookup, or set `case_sensitive_match = false` in the lookup's `transforms.conf` stanza. Search across the last 7 days.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.45.1: Backup restore test evidence — RPO/RTO SLA compliance per ti]
cron_schedule = 0 2 * * 1
dispatch.earliest_time = -7d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.45.1,reg=NIST_800-53
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=backup sourcetype=restore:test earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `rpo_s`, `rpo_slo`, `rto_s`, `rto_slo`, `system`, `test_id`, `tier`. Run:

```spl
index=backup sourcetype=restore:test earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table rpo_s, rpo_slo, rto_s, rto_slo, system, test_id, tier
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(c) Lookup freshness:** The detection joins against `dr_tiers.csv`. Check its modification time and row count:

```spl
| inputlookup dr_tiers.csv
| eval _lookup_row=1
| stats count as rows
```

Compare the row count to the expected baseline; an unexpected drop is a sign the upstream export truncated.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.45.1: NIST 800-53 evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Table Of Tests (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 2 — Line Chart Of Rpo/Rto Trend (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 3 — Single-Value '% Of Tier-1 Systems Within Slo' (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**No results when violations exist:** Check the lookup's case sensitivity (Splunk lookups are case-sensitive by default) and null handling — a null on the input side produces a silent miss, not an error.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**NIST 800-53-specific failure modes:**

• Control baseline selection: NIST 800-53 has Low/Moderate/High/Privacy baselines via FIPS 199 categorisation. A detection written to a High baseline threshold produces false alarms against Low-categorised systems. Join with the system categorisation register `nist_800_53_system_categorization.csv` before thresholding.

• CA-7 continuous monitoring cadence: specifies 'ongoing' authorisation activities. Detections supporting CA-7 must produce metrics at the organisation's ConMon cadence (monthly is common for most agencies) — weekly fires are fine but not themselves evidence of monthly compliance.

• Control inheritance: in a cloud-hybrid deployment, some controls are inherited from the Cloud Service Provider. Detections must not flag 'missing' controls the CSP provides (documented in the SSP via Control Inheritance matrix); reference the SSP appendix and suppress matching findings.

• SP 800-53 Rev 5 restructure: controls were renumbered between Rev 4 and Rev 5. Detections that cite Rev 4 numbering (e.g. AC-2.12) may not match the SSP if the organisation has migrated. Store both Rev numbers in the UC metadata and reconcile.
grandmaExplanation: We help you show backups were tested and restorable when regulators or disaster recovery need proof.
